---
title: "Client Report - Star Wars Survey"
subtitle: "Unit 5 -- Tasks 1, 2 & 3"
author: "Vincent Gentry"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl 
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from lets_plot import *
import polars.selectors as cs
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

data_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"
```

---

# Task 1 -- Data Loading & Email Parsing

## Question 1 — Load the Star Wars Data

So the first thing I noticed is that this CSV file is a mess straight out of the box. There are 10 rows of random metadata at the top before the actual data even starts, and to make things weirder, the column headers are spread across **two separate rows** (rows 11 and 12). Polars can't read this normally, so I had to do a manual cleanup first.

Here's what I did:

1. Read row 11 (the first header row) and used regex to trim down the long survey question text into short, usable names and stored it as series `a`.
2. Read row 12 (the second header row) and cleaned that up too and stored as series `b`.
3. Glued `a + b` together to make the final column names, then read the actual data starting at row 13.

```{python}
# Step 1 — Read and clean the first header row 
a = pl.Series(
    pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=10, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding="utf8-lossy").row(0)
)

# Overwrite the ambiguous "seen any" entry
a[2] = "seen_any"

a = (
    a.str.replace(r".+seen.+", "seen_")
     .str.replace(r".+rank.+", "rank_")
     .str.replace(r"(.+favorably.+)", "fav_")
     .fill_null(strategy="forward")
     .str.replace("\uFFFD+", "")   # remove replacement characters from bad encoding
     .str.strip_chars()
     .str.to_lowercase()
     .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
     .str.replace(r"location(.+)", "location")
)

# Give a few columns more readable names
a[3]  = "star_wars_fan"
a[30] = "who_shot_first"
a[31] = "know_expanded_universe"
a[32] = "expanded_universe_fan"
a[33] = "star_trek_fan"
```

```{python}
# Step 2 — Read and clean the second header row 
b = pl.Series(
    pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=11, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding="utf8-lossy").row(0)
)

b = (
    b.str.replace(r"Star Wars: (Episode \w+) .+", "${1}")
     .str.replace("Response", "")
     .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
     .fill_null("")
     .str.strip_chars()
)
```

```{python}
# Step 3 — Combine headers and load the data
new_names = (a + b).to_list()

df = pl.read_csv(
    data_url,
    skip_rows=12,
    has_header=False,
    new_columns=new_names,
    encoding="utf8-lossy"
)

print(f"Rows: {df.height}  |  Columns: {df.width}")
df.head(3)
```

---

## Question 2 — Extract Names from the Email Column

This one was actually pretty cool once I figured out regex. The emails have people's names baked into them (like `john.smith@gmail.com` or `jane_doe@yahoo.com`), just separated by a `.` or `_` before the `@`. So I used three regex patterns to pick out the first name, last name, and domain from each email. It's basically just telling Python "grab everything before the separator, everything between the separator and the @, and everything after the @."

```{python}
# Q2: Extract first name, last name, and email domain using regex
df_names = df.select([
    pl.col("email"),
    # First name: letters before the first separator (., _, or digit)
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)(?:[._]|\d)", 1).alias("first_name"),
    # Last name: letters after separator (., _, or digits), before @
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"(?:[._]|\d+)([a-zA-Z]+)@", 1).alias("last_name"),
    # Domain: everything after @, stripped of whitespace
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"@(.+)$", 1).str.strip_chars().alias("email_domain"),
])

# Show the first 5 rows
df_names.head(5)
```

The table above shows just the first 5 rows. you can see the email gets split up into the three new columns pretty cleanly. The regex handles both the dot and underscore separator styles, which covers basically everyone in the dataset.

---

## Question 3 — Domain Count Table

For this one I just needed to count how many people used each email domain. One small gotcha was that some domains were capitalized inconsistently (like `Gmail.com` vs `gmail.com`), so I converted everything to lowercase first before grouping. That way they all get counted together instead of as separate domains.

```{python}
# Q3: Count respondents per email domain (case-insensitive)
domain_counts = (
    df.select(
        pl.col("email")
          .str.extract(r"@(.+)$", 1)
          .str.strip_chars()
          .str.to_lowercase()
          .alias("email_domain")
    )
    .group_by("email_domain")
    .agg(pl.len().alias("count"))
    .sort("count", descending=True)
)

domain_counts
```

The table is sorted from most to least used. Looks like **`{python} domain_counts["email_domain"][0]`** is the most popular domain with **`{python} domain_counts["count"][0]`** respondents. Which is not too surprising honestly.

---

# Task 2 -- Recoding Range Variables

---

## Question 1: Filter to Respondents Who Have Seen at Least One Film

So the instructions specifically say NOT to use the `seen_any` column even though that sounds like exactly what you'd want. Instead, I'm looking at the six individual per-movie columns, one for each episode. If any of those are filled in, the person has seen at least one film and they stay in. If all six are null, they skipped the whole section which probably means they haven't seen any, so they get dropped.

```{python}
# The six per-episode "have you seen this film" columns
seen_cols = [f"seen_Episode_{ep}" for ep in ["I", "II", "III", "IV", "V", "VI"]]

# Keep rows where at least one episode column is not null
df_seen = df.filter(
    pl.any_horizontal([pl.col(c).is_not_null() for c in seen_cols])
)

print(f"Filtered dataset — Rows: {df_seen.height}  (expected: 835)")
df_seen.select(["star_wars_fan", "seen_any"] + seen_cols).head(5)
```

After the filter I'm left with **`{python} df_seen.height`** rows, which is exactly 835 like the task says it should be. Good sign that the logic worked.

---

## Question 2: Recode Range Variables for Machine Learning

This is the main thing this task is about. Machine learning models can't do anything with strings like `"30-44"` or `"$50,000 - $99,999"`. Those need to become actual numbers. So I'm going through each of the problem columns and converting them. For ranges I'm using the **midpoint** of the range as the number (so `"18-29"` becomes `23.5`), and for ordered categories like education or favorability I'm using **ordinal encoding** where each level gets a number in order. After making the new numeric column I delete the old string one since we don't need it anymore.

---

### 2a: Age

There are only four age buckets so this one's pretty simple. I just picked the midpoint for each range. The last one `"> 60"` is open-ended so there's no real midpoint, so I went with 65 which seemed like a fair middle-of-the-road guess for that group.

```{python}
age_map = {
    "18-29": 23.5,
    "30-44": 37.0,
    "45-60": 52.5,
    "> 60":  65.0,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("age")
      .replace(age_map, return_dtype=pl.Float64)
      .alias("age_num")
).drop("age")

print("age_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["age_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))
```

---

### 2b: Education

Education is a natural ladder from least to most schooling, so I'm just numbering the levels 1 through 5. No diploma at the bottom gets a 1, grad degree at the top gets a 5. Simple enough.

```{python}
edu_map = {
    "Less than high school degree":       1,
    "High school degree":                 2,
    "Some college or Associate degree":   3,
    "Bachelor degree":                    4,
    "Graduate degree":                    5,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("education")
      .replace(edu_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32)
      .alias("education_num")
).drop("education")

print("education_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["education_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))
```

---

### 2c: Household Income

Same idea as age, just use the midpoint of each bracket. The tricky one is `"$150,000+"` since it has no upper end. I went with $175,000 as a rough estimate. It's not perfect but it's better than leaving it as a string or just using 150,000 which would underrepresent that group.

```{python}
income_map = {
    "$0 - $24,999":          12_500,
    "$25,000 - $49,999":     37_500,
    "$50,000 - $99,999":     75_000,
    "$100,000 - $149,999":  125_000,
    "$150,000+":            175_000,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("household_income")
      .replace(income_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32)
      .alias("income_num")
).drop("household_income")

print("income_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["income_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))
```

---

### 2d: Favorability Ratings

This one was actually kind of satisfying to figure out. There are 14 favorability columns (one per character), and they ALL use the exact same 6-level scale, so I don't have to write the same map 14 times. I can just loop over all the columns that start with `fav_` and apply the same replacement to each one in a single pass.

I set `"Unfamiliar (N/A)"` to 0 instead of null because "I don't know who this is" is meaningfully different from hating someone. It seemed wrong to just drop those responses.

```{python}
fav_map = {
    "Unfamiliar (N/A)":                             0,
    "Very unfavorably":                             1,
    "Somewhat unfavorably":                         2,
    "Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)":  3,
    "Somewhat favorably":                           4,
    "Very favorably":                               5,
}

fav_cols = [c for c in df_seen.columns if c.startswith("fav_")]

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns([
    pl.col(c).replace(fav_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32).alias(c + "_num")
    for c in fav_cols
]).drop(fav_cols)

print(f"Replaced {len(fav_cols)} fav_ columns.")
print("Sample fav_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["fav_Han_Solo_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))
```

---

### Reformatted Data Excerpt

Here's what the data looks like now after all four recoding steps. I picked a handful of the new numeric columns to show alongside `star_wars_fan` just so it's clear what we're working with.

```{python}
# Show a selection of the new numeric columns
preview_cols = [
    "star_wars_fan", "age_num", "education_num", "income_num",
    "fav_Han_Solo_num", "fav_Luke_Skywalker_num", "fav_Darth_Vader_num",
    "fav_Yoda_num"
]

df_seen.select(preview_cols).head(10)
```

**What changed:**

| Original column | New column | How I encoded it |
|---|---|---|
| `age` | `age_num` | Midpoint of each age range (23.5 / 37.0 / 52.5 / 65.0) |
| `education` | `education_num` | Ordinal 1–5 (1 = no diploma, 5 = grad degree) |
| `household_income` | `income_num` | Midpoint of each income bracket (12,500 → 175,000) |
| `fav_*` (14 cols) | `fav_*_num` (14 cols) | Ordinal 0–5 (0 = never heard of them, 5 = love them) |

All the original string columns got dropped right after their replacement was created. The dataset should be in way better shape for a model now.

---

# Task 3 -- Machine Learning Model

## Executive Summary

- **Goal:** The goal of this project is to clean up the FiveThirtyEight Star Wars survey data, verify it matches the original article, and build a machine learning model that predicts whether someone makes at least $50k a year.
- **Data:** About 1,186 people were surveyed in 2014 about Star Wars. The dataset includes which movies they've seen, how they ranked them, opinions on characters, and some basic demographic info. Fake names and emails were added to the dataset for this project.
- **Data Prep:** I dropped the ID, name, and email columns since they aren't useful for modeling. I created a yes/no target column from the income column (1 = $50k+, 0 = less than $50k). All text columns were one-hot encoded and missing values were filled in.
- **Validation:** I remade two charts from the original article to check that the dataset looks right.
- **Model:** A Random Forest model hit above 55% accuracy on the test set.


## Methods

To make sure our data is correct, I recreated two charts from the original FiveThirtyEight article. If they look similar, we can trust the data.

**Chart 1 — Films seen (among Star Wars fans)**

```{python}
# Keep only respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film
fans = df.filter(pl.col("seen_any") == "Yes")
n_fans = len(fans)

seen_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("seen_") and c != "seen_any"]

# Count non-null values per seen column (non-null = "Yes, they saw it")
seen_counts = pl.DataFrame({
    "film_key": seen_cols,
    "n_seen": [fans[c].drop_nulls().len() for c in seen_cols]
}).with_columns(
    (pl.col("n_seen") / n_fans * 100).alias("pct_seen"),
    pl.col("film_key")
      .str.replace("seen_", "")
      .str.replace_all("_", " ")
      .alias("film")
).sort("film_key")

(
    ggplot(seen_counts, aes(x="pct_seen", y="film"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#4393c3")
    + labs(
        title="Which Star Wars films have you seen?",
        subtitle="% of fans (respondents who have seen any film)",
        x="% of respondents",
        y=""
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

**Chart 2 — Average film ranking (1 = best, 6 = worst)**

```{python}
rank_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("rank_")]

rank_avgs = pl.DataFrame({
    "film_key": rank_cols,
    "avg_rank": [
        df.select(
            pl.col(c).cast(pl.Float64, strict=False)
        ).mean().item()
        for c in rank_cols
    ]
}).with_columns(
    pl.col("film_key")
      .str.replace("rank_", "")
      .str.replace_all("_", " ")
      .alias("film")
).sort("avg_rank")

(
    ggplot(rank_avgs, aes(x="avg_rank", y="film"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#d6604d")
    + labs(
        title="Average film ranking",
        subtitle="Lower score = ranked more favorably (1 = best, 6 = worst)",
        x="Average rank",
        y=""
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

```{python}
# ── Step 1: Identify and drop non-feature columns ──────────────────────────
drop_cols = [
    c for c in df.columns
    if any(kw in c.lower() for kw in ["respondentid", "first_name", "last_name", "email"])
]
print("Columns dropped (ID / name / email):", drop_cols)
```

```{python}
# ── Step 2: Create binary target from household_income ─────────────────────
income_50k_vals = ["$50,000 - $99,999", "$100,000 - $149,999", "$150,000+"]

df_ml = (
    df.drop(drop_cols)
      .filter(pl.col("household_income").is_not_null())   # drop rows with no income label
      .with_columns(
          pl.when(pl.col("household_income").is_in(income_50k_vals))
            .then(pl.lit(1))
            .otherwise(pl.lit(0))
            .alias("target")
      )
)

print("Target distribution:")
print(df_ml["target"].value_counts().sort("target"))
```

```{python}
# ── Step 3: Separate features and target; drop original income column ───────
# household_income is now encoded in `target` — keeping it as a feature
# would make the prediction trivial, so we remove it.
target = df_ml["target"].to_numpy()
feature_df = df_ml.drop(["household_income", "target"])

# Identify column types for the ColumnTransformer
numeric_cols = feature_df.select(cs.numeric()).columns
cat_cols     = feature_df.select(cs.string()).columns

print(f"Numeric features : {len(numeric_cols)}")
print(f"Categorical features: {len(cat_cols)}")
print(f"Total rows after filtering: {len(feature_df)}")
```

```{python}
# ── Step 4: Convert to pandas (via dict — no pyarrow required) 
# sklearn requires pandas DataFrames or numpy arrays; pyarrow is not installed,
# so we use a dict-of-lists conversion which handles nulls as None → NaN.
X = pd.DataFrame({col: feature_df[col].to_list() for col in feature_df.columns})
```

- **Number columns** — filled in with the median of that column.
- **Text columns** — filled in with the most common value in that column.


## Results

```{python}
# ── Build preprocessing + classification pipeline
numeric_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median"))
])

categorical_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
    ("onehot", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False))
])

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ("num", numeric_transformer, numeric_cols),
    ("cat", categorical_transformer, cat_cols)
])

pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("classifier", RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42))
])
```

```{python}
# ── Train / test split and model fitting ───────────────────────────────────
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, target, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=target
)

pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = pipeline.predict(X_test)
```

```{python}
# ── Accuracy and classification report ────────────────────────────────────
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print(f"Test Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}  ({accuracy * 100:.1f}%)\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["< $50k", ">= $50k"]))
```

```{python}
# ── Feature importance (top 20) ───────────────────────────────────────────
rf_model = pipeline.named_steps["classifier"]
ohe = pipeline.named_steps["preprocessor"].named_transformers_["cat"].named_steps["onehot"]

# Reconstruct feature names after one-hot encoding
cat_feature_names = ohe.get_feature_names_out(cat_cols).tolist()
all_feature_names = list(numeric_cols) + cat_feature_names

importance_df = pl.DataFrame({
    "feature": all_feature_names,
    "importance": rf_model.feature_importances_.tolist()
}).sort("importance", descending=True).head(20)

(
    ggplot(importance_df, aes(x="importance", y="feature"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#2ca02c")
    + labs(
        title="Top 20 Feature Importances",
        subtitle="Random Forest — predicting household income ≥ $50k",
        x="Mean decrease in impurity",
        y=""
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```


## Conclusion

The Random Forest model got above 55% accuracy, which meets the project goal. The most useful features for predicting income were things like age and education, which makes sense. Star Wars opinions didn't matter as much, but they did help a little.

A few things to keep in mind though:

- The survey is from 2014, so the data is pretty old and income patterns have probably changed.
- Some people didn't answer the income question, so I had to remove those rows. If lower-income people were more likely to skip it, that could skew the model.
- The model could probably be improved with more data or by tuning the settings.

Overall this was a good exercise in cleaning messy survey data and building a full ML pipeline from scratch.

---

# Stretch Task -- Chart Replication & Validation

## Data Loading & Cleaning

The data cleaning mirrors the approach from Task 3 — the two-row header is merged
into a single set of column names before the data rows are read in.

```{python}
a = pl.Series(
    pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=10, n_rows=1, has_header=False,
                encoding="utf8-lossy").row(0)
)

a[2] = "seen_any"

a = (
    a.str.replace(r".+seen.+",       "seen_")
     .str.replace(r".+rank.+",       "rank_")
     .str.replace(r"(.+favorably.+)", "fav_")
     .fill_null(strategy="forward")
     .str.replace("\uFFFD+", "")
     .str.strip_chars()
     .str.to_lowercase()
     .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
     .str.replace(r"location(.+)", "location")
)

a[3]  = "star_wars_fan"
a[30] = "who_shot_first"
a[31] = "know_expanded_universe"
a[32] = "expanded_universe_fan"
a[33] = "star_trek_fan"

b = pl.Series(
    pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=11, n_rows=1, has_header=False,
                encoding="utf8-lossy").row(0)
)

b = (
    b.str.replace(r"Star Wars: (Episode \w+) .+", "${1}")
     .str.replace("Response", "")
     .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
     .fill_null("")
     .str.strip_chars()
)

new_names = (a + b).to_list()

df = pl.read_csv(
    data_url, skip_rows=12, has_header=False,
    new_columns=new_names
)

print(f"Rows: {len(df)}  |  Columns: {len(df.columns)}")
```

---

## Chart 1 — "Which 'Star Wars' Films Have You Seen?"


```{python}
fans = df.filter(pl.col("seen_any") == "Yes")
n_fans = len(fans)

seen_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("seen_") and c != "seen_any"]

episode_labels = {
    "seen_Episode_I":   "The Phantom Menace",
    "seen_Episode_II":  "Attack of the Clones",
    "seen_Episode_III": "Revenge of the Sith",
    "seen_Episode_IV":  "A New Hope",
    "seen_Episode_V":   "The Empire Strikes Back",
    "seen_Episode_VI":  "Return of the Jedi",
}

seen_counts = pl.DataFrame({
    "col":   seen_cols,
    "n_seen": [fans[c].drop_nulls().len() for c in seen_cols],
}).with_columns(
    (pl.col("n_seen") / n_fans * 100).alias("pct"),
    pl.col("col").replace(episode_labels).alias("film"),
).sort("pct", descending=True).with_columns(
    (pl.col("pct").round(0).cast(pl.Int32).cast(pl.String) + "%").alias("label")
)

print(seen_counts.select(["film", "pct"]))
```

```{python}
(
    ggplot(seen_counts, aes(x="pct", y=as_discrete("film", order_by="pct")))
    + geom_bar(
        stat="identity",
        fill="#008fd5",
        width=0.6,
    )
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="label"),
        hjust=-0.1,
        size=9,
        color="#555555",
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(
        limits=[0, 95],
        breaks=[0, 20, 40, 60, 80],
        labels=["0%", "20%", "40%", "60%", "80%"],
    )
    + labs(
        title="Which 'Star Wars' Films Have You Seen?",
        subtitle=f"Of {n_fans:,} respondents who have seen any film",
        x="",
        y="",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_x=element_line(color="#e0e0e0"),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_ticks=element_blank(),
        axis_text=element_text(size=10),
    )
)
```

---

## Chart 2 — "What's the Best 'Star Wars' Movie?

```{python}
seen_all = fans.filter(
    pl.all_horizontal(
        [pl.col(c).is_not_null() for c in seen_cols]
    )
)
n_all = len(seen_all)

rank_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("rank_")]

episode_rank_labels = {
    "rank_Episode_I":   "The Phantom Menace",
    "rank_Episode_II":  "Attack of the Clones",
    "rank_Episode_III": "Revenge of the Sith",
    "rank_Episode_IV":  "A New Hope",
    "rank_Episode_V":   "The Empire Strikes Back",
    "rank_Episode_VI":  "Return of the Jedi",
}

ranked_best = pl.DataFrame({
    "col": rank_cols,
    "n_best": [
        seen_all.filter(
            pl.col(c).cast(pl.Int32, strict=False) == 1
        ).shape[0]
        for c in rank_cols
    ],
}).with_columns(
    (pl.col("n_best") / n_all * 100).alias("pct_best"),
    pl.col("col").replace(episode_rank_labels).alias("film"),
).sort("pct_best", descending=True).with_columns(
    (pl.col("pct_best").round(0).cast(pl.Int32).cast(pl.String) + "%").alias("label")
)

print(ranked_best.select(["film", "pct_best"]))
```

```{python}
(
    ggplot(ranked_best, aes(x="pct_best", y=as_discrete("film", order_by="pct_best")))
    + geom_bar(
        stat="identity",
        fill="#008fd5",
        width=0.6,
    )
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="label"),
        hjust=-0.1,
        size=9,
        color="#555555",
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(
        limits=[0, 55],
        breaks=[0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
        labels=["0%", "10%", "20%", "30%", "40%", "50%"],
    )
    + labs(
        title="What's the Best 'Star Wars' Movie?",
        subtitle=f"Of {n_all:,} respondents who have seen all 6 films, % who ranked each film #1",
        x="",
        y="",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_x=element_line(color="#e0e0e0"),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_ticks=element_blank(),
        axis_text=element_text(size=10),
    )
)
```


---

## Validation

### Chart 1 — Films Seen

Pretty much what you'd expect, the original trilogy (Episodes IV–VI) was seen by around 79–83% of fans, and the prequels drop off to somewhere in the 53–60% range. Our chart looks basically the same as the one in the article, so the data checks out here.

### Chart 2 — Best Film

*The Empire Strikes Back* is the most popular, grabbing about 36% of #1 votes, with *A New Hope* not too far behind at around 25%. The prequels are basically a rounding error. That matches the article pretty closely, so the ranking data looks good too.

### Overall

Both charts line up well enough with the article that we can trust the dataset. The numbers aren't going to be pixel-perfect since these are survey estimates, but the story they tell is the same.
